---
title: "Deep Dive Expression: compiling ncRNA machinery fastas"
author: "Kathleen Durkin"
date: "2026-04-13"
categories: ["E5-coral"]
format:
  html:
    toc: true
execute: 
  eval: TRUE
engine: knitr
---

In my last post I used GLM 5.1 (via OpenWork) to compile a list of protein machinery important to miRNA and lncRNA biogenesis and function. I've synthesized this to an Excel sheet of proteins and associated gene names, `ncRNA_machinery.xlsx`. It and all other files associated with this post are available at `./output/2026_04_13_DDE_ncRNA_machinery_fastas`

Now I need protein fastas for all of these, preferably from a close relative of our coral species. To get these, I'll be using the [Uniprot protein database](https://www.uniprot.org/) with the following searching approach:

Use search parameters to limit results to Cnidarians, and to exclude fragmentary/incomplete sequences. I also restricted to proteins that were identified based on evidence at protein level, evidence at transcript level, or based on homology -- this will *exclude* proteins with a predicted or uncertain identification basis.

> (taxonomy_id:6073) AND (fragment:false) NOT (existence:4) NOT (existence:5)

I then included search terms for each protein/gene, generally in the format:

> ((protein_name:PROT_ABBRV) OR (gene:GENE_ABBRV))

This was occasionally modified to also include a search for full protein or gene names (for example, "Exportin-5", in addition to "XPO5"). This was sometimes necessary because many sequences are not annotated with the "standard" gene name.

I then downloaded sequence results in `.fasta` and `.fasta.gz` file formats.

Some of the selected proteins weren't annotated in any cnidarians, so I instead searched in bilaterians and restricted to only reviewed entries. These are highlighted in the `ncRNA_machinery.xlsx` file.

Unzip all .fasta.gz files, then combine all fastas

```{bash, eval=FALSE}
# Unzip all .gz files
gunzip ./output/2026_04_13_DDE_ncRNA_machinery_fastas/*.fasta.gz

# Combine all fasta files into one
cat ./output/2026_04_13_DDE_ncRNA_machinery_fastas/*.fasta > ./output/2026_04_13_DDE_ncRNA_machinery_fastas/ncRNA_machinery_unfiltered.fasta
```

```{bash}
cd ./output/2026_04_13_DDE_ncRNA_machinery_fastas/

grep -c '^>' ncRNA_machinery_unfiltered.fasta

grep '^>' ncRNA_machinery_unfiltered.fasta | head -3
```

Finally, most of the protein sequences have non-standard gene names as a consequence of using unreviewed Trembl entries from cnidarians (e.g., using source-specific names for transcripts, like "LOC###"). We'll probably want standardized gene names down the like, since full protein names can be lengthy. As far as I'm aware, there's no good way to look up protein names and obtain corresponding gene names in a standardized way, so I think I have to fall back on manual curation. I used Claude Opus 4.6 to help me with this task of parsing protein names to obtain standardized gene names, and obtained an R script for this purpose: `standardize_gene_names.R`

```{bash}
cd ./output/2026_04_13_DDE_ncRNA_machinery_fastas/

Rscript standardize_gene_names.R ncRNA_machinery_unfiltered.fasta ncRNA_machinery.fasta
```

Great! The manually-corrected fasta has the same number of sequences, no unmapped sequences, the original gene names (GN:) were not overwritten, and each sequence now contains a curated gene name field (CGN:) with standardized gene names.

So I now have a final fasta file containing protein sequences for ncRNA protein machinery! The majority of sequences were obtained from cnidarians, but a small subset weren't annotated in any cnidarians and were instead sourced from reviewed bilaterian entries.

I'll now use this fasta, in combination with the epimachinery fasta provided by Hollie and Steven to look for epigenetic protein machinery in the miRNA targets of our DDE data (and probably in the timeseries data in the future)
